topic: W001 sign-flip paragraph post-EXP-137 integration: enhanced steel-man, direction_correct paradox, precision derivation
triggered_by: Priority queue item 68: EXP-137 integrated — W001 sign-flip paragraph enhanced with dome verbatim rationale, noise_floor absence, direction_correct paradox, explicit precision derivation
our_argument_summary: The W001 sign-flip paragraph in SEC-2b.7 argues that W001's −2.1 nT prediction was unfalsifiable by construction because it sits below the instrument noise floor. The paragraph (a) steel-mans the dome's defense with a verbatim quote ('Instrument noise floor prevents measurement...'), (b) notes W001's results.json entry lacks a noise_floor value unlike W004, (c) identifies the direction_correct computation paradox (dome evaluates and stores direction_correct: false but doesn't count it), (d) makes the precision derivation explicit (|3.73 − (−2.1)| / 7.2875 ≈ 0.80 nT), and (e) distinguishes between incidental and systematic below-noise predictions. This is a substantial improvement over the c2-reviewed text, which conflated sigma_distance with SNR and failed to preempt the dome's defense.

c2_issues_resolution.sigma_distance_vs_snr_conflation: FIXED. The c2 major issue was that the text treated sigma_distance 7.3 as a detection confidence metric. The new text correctly frames it as prediction uncertainty and leads with structural unfalsifiability rather than 'detection of the wrong answer.' The old rhetorical punch line ('A 7-sigma deviation with the wrong sign is not a non-detection — it is a detection of the wrong answer') is gone, replaced by the structural argument.
c2_issues_resolution.noise_floor_value_absent: FIXED. Parenthetical added noting W001's results.json lacks a noise_floor value, with cross-reference to W004's 4.4 nT.
c2_issues_resolution.dome_defense_not_preempted: FIXED. Verbatim dome rationale included and explicitly engaged with ('This is the strongest version of the defense, and it deserves direct engagement').
c2_issues_resolution.overrides_language_imprecise: FIXED. 'Escape hatch overrides the direction check' replaced with precise mechanism description: 'classifies any result with snr_sufficient: false as below_detection_threshold regardless of what direction_correct reports.'
c2_issues_resolution.diagnostic_difference_claim_imprecise: FIXED. Old strawman distinction removed. New text distinguishes between 'predictions that happen to land near noise incidentally' vs 'predictions systematically placed below the noise floor.'

holes_found.description: The closing sentence distinguishes between 'predictions that happen to land near noise incidentally and predictions systematically placed below the noise floor.' The word 'systematically' implies a deliberate pattern across multiple predictions. But the text only documents ONE below-noise prediction (W001). The dome lists two below-detection-threshold entries (W001, W004), and W004 actually scored confirmed_strong in results.json — so W004 is not even a consistent example of the same pattern. With n=1 examples of below-noise predictions being shielded from falsification, 'systematically' is an overstatement. A dome defender says: 'One prediction falling below noise is not a system. Our model predicts what the physics gives us. If the lunar transit effect at Huancayo is genuinely ~2 nT, that is what the equations produce. We do not control instrument noise floors. Show me five below-noise predictions before you call it systematic.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) document additional below-noise predictions to substantiate the pattern claim, or (b) soften 'systematically placed' to something like 'placed below the noise floor' without the adverb. The structural argument (sub-noise predictions are unfalsifiable by construction) does not require proving deliberate systematic placement — it holds even for a single prediction. The overstatement weakens an otherwise airtight argument.

holes_found.description: The text argues: 'The dome's code disagrees with the dome's classification: it considers direction evaluable enough to compute but not evaluable enough to count.' This conflates computational triviality with epistemic reliability. Computing direction_correct requires only checking whether the observed value's sign matches the prediction's sign — a trivial operation on any nonzero number. The dome's below_detection_threshold classification is about whether the OBSERVED VALUE reliably represents the physical signal versus noise fluctuation. These are different questions: 'Is 3.73 > 0?' (always answerable) vs 'Does 3.73 nT represent a real positive magnetic signal or a noise artifact?' (depends on SNR). A dome defender says: 'We compute every diagnostic field because complete data is good engineering. We do not claim direction is meaningful below noise — we log it anyway because logging is free and discarding data is not. Your inference that computing a field means we consider it reliable is a non sequitur.' The anticipated objection in EXP-137 counters this by noting direction_correct is part of the verdict formula (pass = direction_correct AND snr_sufficient), not incidental logging. This is a solid rebuttal but it does not appear in the published text — only in the expansion's anticipated_objections. The published text is vulnerable to the 'good engineering' defense.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence after the paradox claim: 'The field is not incidental logging — it is one of two inputs to the auto_verdict formula (pass = direction_correct AND snr_sufficient). The dome's scoring code treats it as verdict-relevant, then discards it when it returns the wrong answer.' This is already in EXP-137's anticipated_objections and would close the escape hatch.

holes_found.description: The text states: 'The dome states this directly: "Instrument noise floor prevents measurement. Prediction remains structurally valid. Hardware limitation, not model failure."' EXP-137 sources this to 'wins.html.' However, the raw-text snapshots in the repository contain shorter formulations: raw-text/02-wins.txt has 'W001 — Noise Floor Limit (instrument limitation, not model failure)' and raw-text/05-model.txt has 'W001 and W004 are below detection threshold, not model failures.' Neither matches the full verbatim quote. If the dome updates its wins.html, the quoted text may no longer appear there, and our raw-text archive doesn't back it up. A defender could claim misquotation if the exact wording has changed.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a footnote citing the specific URL/commit/date where the verbatim text was captured, or (b) verify the quote is still present on the dome's live site and update raw-text to capture it. If the quote cannot be verified, replace with a close paraphrase sourced to our raw-text: 'The dome classifies W001 as an "instrument limitation, not model failure" — a noise floor problem, not a prediction failure.'

holes_found.description: The text uses W004's 4.4 nT noise floor as a proxy for W001's unknown noise floor. The parenthetical rightly notes W001 doesn't publish its own value. However, the text does not acknowledge that W004 is a different observation entirely (2024 Eclipse 9-Station Replication) at different stations under different conditions. A technically literate reader may wonder: why is an eclipse observation's noise floor relevant to a lunar transit at Huancayo? The argument holds at any noise floor above ~2.1 nT, so the specific W004 value is not load-bearing — but the text treats it as a reference point without acknowledging the cross-station limitation.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief qualifier: 'W004's published noise floor of 4.4 nT sets a reference measurement scale — the specific threshold at Huancayo may differ, but the argument holds at any noise floor exceeding the 2.1 nT prediction magnitude.' This makes the argument robust to any noise floor value.

holes_found.description: The text says the prediction precision 'is five times smaller than the instruments' noise floor.' The actual ratio is 4.4 / 0.80 = 5.5x. 'Five times' is close but slightly understates the gap. A minor imprecision that a dome defender is unlikely to exploit, but given we criticize the dome for sloppy arithmetic, we should be exact.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'five times' to 'over five times' or 'roughly five-and-a-half times.' Alternatively, keep 'five times' and add the numbers in parentheses: '(0.80 nT vs 4.4 nT).' The latter is arguably better because readers can verify the ratio themselves.

stronger_arguments.description: The direction_correct paradox would be much stronger if the text included the specific rebuttal to the 'good engineering' defense. EXP-137 anticipated this objection perfectly: direction_correct is not incidental logging — it is half of the verdict formula (pass = direction_correct AND snr_sufficient). The dome's own code architecture treats it as a verdict input, then ignores it when snr_sufficient=false. If the field were truly diagnostic, the verdict logic would not reference it. This rebuttal is in EXP-137's anticipated_objections but was not included in the published paragraph.

The paragraph could strengthen its case by noting that W001 has monitoring classification 'none' in the code_analysis_tags — monitor.py contains no validation domain for W001 at all. The only quantitative data for W001 comes from results.json, which is now 404. So there is no live monitoring, no archived monitoring, and the only structured scoring artifact (results.json) has been removed. The prediction is not just unfalsifiable by construction — it is now unverifiable by anyone.
sources: Code analysis tags from curmudgeon reviews

kernel_of_truth.description: The EXP-137 rewrite is a substantial improvement. The argument now leads with structural unfalsifiability rather than the c2-vulnerable 'detection of the wrong answer' framing. The verbatim dome quote followed by 'it deserves direct engagement' is excellent intellectual honesty. The noise_floor absence note and precision derivation are new forensic details that strengthen the case. The overall quality is high — this paragraph is now one of the more technically rigorous pieces of the review.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The improvements don't eliminate all vulnerabilities. The 'systematically placed' language overstates the evidence (n=1), the direction_correct paradox is vulnerable to the 'good engineering' defense without the verdict-formula rebuttal, and the verbatim quote is not backed by our raw-text archive. These are moderate issues, not critical — the core argument is sound.

recommended_edits.description: Add verdict-formula rebuttal to close direction_correct 'good engineering' escape hatch
recommended_edits.current: The dome's code disagrees with the dome's classification: it considers direction evaluable enough to compute but not evaluable enough to count.
recommended_edits.proposed: The dome's code disagrees with the dome's classification: it considers direction evaluable enough to compute but not evaluable enough to count. And direction_correct is not incidental logging — it is one of two inputs to the auto_verdict formula (pass = direction_correct AND snr_sufficient). The dome's scoring architecture treats it as verdict-relevant, then discards it when it returns the wrong answer.

recommended_edits.description: Soften 'systematically' to avoid overstatement with n=1 evidence
recommended_edits.current: predictions systematically placed below the noise floor
recommended_edits.proposed: predictions calibrated below the noise floor

recommended_edits.description: Add cross-station qualifier for W004 noise floor proxy
recommended_edits.current: W004's published noise floor of 4.4 nT sets the measurement scale
recommended_edits.proposed: W004's published noise floor of 4.4 nT provides a reference measurement scale (the specific threshold at Huancayo may differ, but the argument holds at any noise floor exceeding the 2.1 nT prediction magnitude)

tldr_review.tldr_text: The dome's results.json silently migrated from 33/34 passing (97.0%) to 35/35 (100.0%) with no changelog. Of 35 entries, 32 have no quantitative data — bulk-imported catalog WINs rubber-stamped as confirmed. The 3 entries with real data each exhibit a scoring pathology: pred=obs with zero noise (TASK-3-1), a win-tab/API contradiction on the same observation (W004), and a sign-wrong miss excluded from the failure count (W001). The 100% is unfalsifiable by design.
tldr_review.tldr_notes: The TLDR covers the full SEC-2b.7, not just the W001 paragraph. Its W001 reference ('a sign-wrong miss excluded from the failure count') is compatible with the new paragraph's structural framing, though the TLDR emphasizes the observational fact (sign-wrong miss) while the paragraph emphasizes the structural mechanism (unfalsifiable by construction). This simplification is appropriate for a TLDR — it is not misleading.

citations_verified: W001 predicted −2.1 nT — confirmed from ISS-705-verification.json
W001 observed +3.73 nT — confirmed from ISS-705-verification.json
sigma_distance 7.2875 — confirmed; text uses both 7.3 (rounded) and 7.2875 (exact) appropriately
W004 noise_floor: 4.4 nT — confirmed from ISS-705-verification.json
Precision derivation: |3.73 − (−2.1)| / 7.2875 = 5.83 / 7.2875 ≈ 0.80 nT — verified by independent computation
citations_unchecked: Verbatim dome quote: 'Instrument noise floor prevents measurement. Prediction remains structurally valid. Hardware limitation, not model failure.' — EXP-137 sources to wins.html but this exact text is not in our raw-text/02-wins.txt or raw-text/05-model.txt snapshots. The raw-text has shorter formulations ('instrument limitation, not model failure'). May be accurate but unverifiable from archived material.
W001 results.json entry lacks noise_floor — sourced to ISS-705-verification.json; the claim that W001 lacks the field while W004 has it should be independently verified

advocate_mode.best_dome_defense: The dome's strongest defense against this paragraph is the 'physics gives what it gives' argument: the lunar transit magnetic effect at Huancayo IS predicted to be ~2 nT by the dome's equations. If the physics predicts a small signal, the prediction must be small. Inflating it above the noise floor to make it testable would be scientific fraud. The dome did not choose to make W001 sub-noise — the model's equations produced that value. Furthermore, the dome's v45 evolution history (raw-text/06-evolution.txt) shows the model ORIGINALLY counted W001 as falsified, then introduced below_detection_threshold granularity in v48-v49 specifically to handle the noise-floor issue more honestly. This is the dome IMPROVING its methodology, not hiding a failure. Our text frames the threshold classification as a 'falsification shield' but the dome's version history frames it as an honest methodological upgrade.
advocate_mode.defense_neutralized_by: The paragraph's structural argument survives: even if the 2.1 nT prediction is what the physics requires, the dome still cannot claim this as a confirmed WIN or count it in its success statistics. The dome evolution history actually helps our argument — moving from 'falsified' to 'below_detection_threshold' is upgrading the W001 status from failure to neutral, not from failure to better methodology. If the prediction is genuinely untestable, the honest classification is 'untestable' (not in the WIN count), not 'below detection threshold' (implying the prediction is valid but unmeasurable). The dome puts W001 in a category that preserves its structural validity ('Prediction remains structurally valid') rather than acknowledging it as an untestable prediction that should not count for or against the model.
advocate_mode.remaining_vulnerability: The 'good engineering' defense against the direction_correct paradox is the most locally effective dome counter. Without the verdict-formula rebuttal in the published text, a technically savvy defender can plausibly dismiss the paradox as overinterpretation of logging practices.

deeper_analysis:
This review compares the post-EXP-137 text against the c2 review's recommendations and finds the integration substantially successful. The c2 review's six identified issues have all been addressed. The new text is significantly stronger than its predecessor — leading with structural unfalsifiability, steel-manning the dome's defense, adding forensic details (noise_floor absence, precision derivation), and introducing the direction_correct computation paradox.

The remaining vulnerabilities are moderate, not critical. The most impactful fix would be adding the verdict-formula rebuttal ('direction_correct is one of two inputs to auto_verdict, not incidental logging') which is already written in EXP-137's anticipated_objections but was not included in the published text. This is a one-sentence addition that would close the most significant remaining escape hatch.

On the other side of the argument: the dome's evolution history (v45 counted W001 as falsified, v48+ introduced below_detection_threshold) is genuinely interesting context that cuts both ways. From the dome's perspective, this shows methodological improvement — acknowledging noise-floor limitations rather than crudely classifying sub-noise results as failures. From our perspective, it shows the dome upgrading a failure to a non-count, preserving the 0 falsified headline. Both readings are defensible. The current text does not discuss the version history, which means it misses both the dome's strongest defense-in-depth ('we improved our methodology') and our strongest counter ('you reclassified a failure as non-counted'). This could be a future expansion target.

Overall assessment: the paragraph is now one of the more technically rigorous pieces in the review. The minor edits recommended would bring it from 'strong' to 'bulletproof.'
